label: delphi-r2-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 80,
  "confidence": "high",
  "position_summary": "A court applying City of San Jose v. Superior Court would very likely hold that the Signal messages \u2014 discussions by sitting councilmembers of franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will realistically accept,\" and RFP timing \u2014 meet the content-based definition of \"public records\" under Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e) regardless of their location on personal devices/accounts, and would very likely find the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer deficient because the city never inquired of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications before answering, a search-adequacy failure San Jose's own remand contemplated. The proposition is well-supported but not certain, mainly because the auto-deletion of most messages complicates how definitively a court can characterize the destroyed content versus the ~40 preserved screenshots.",
  "rationales": [
    "San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes public-records status turn on whether content 'relates to the conduct of the public's business,' not on the account or device where it is stored, and the stipulated subject matter here (franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing) sits at the core of that test rather than its margins.",
    "Because three sitting councilmembers were direct participants discussing a matter pending before their own body, this presents the strong-content-nexus scenario San Jose treated as clearly covered, unlike the harder edge cases (e.g., low-level staff or third-party communications) the Court left more open.",
    "San Jose's remand instructions and the CPRA duty-to-assist framework (Gov. Code \u00a7 6253.1) require agencies to make a reasonably calculated inquiry into likely repositories of records, including asking individual officials to check personal accounts when there is reason to believe public business was discussed there; searching only 'city systems' without any such inquiry falls short of that standard as a matter of law.",
    "The ~40 surviving screenshots alone are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed outside city systems at the time of the request, making the 'no responsive records' answer factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the deleted messages.",
    "Some residual uncertainty exists because a court may distinguish between declaring the surviving screenshots public records (clear) and formally characterizing the auto-deleted, unrecoverable messages as having 'been' public records (murkier, resting on witness recollection and inference rather than the writings themselves), which could lead a careful court to hedge the scope of its holding even while finding a search-adequacy violation."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e) (definition of 'public records')",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6253.1 (agency duty to assist requesters)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 (records retention and destruction)",
    "Consider verifying whether any post-San Jose trial-court or appellate decisions on remand or in analogous cases have specifically addressed search-adequacy standards for personal-device messaging apps used by elected officials"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will extend a formal 'these were public records' holding to the bulk of auto-deleted messages that no longer exist and can only be characterized through witness recollection and the 40 surviving screenshots, versus limiting its clear holding to the preserved screenshots and treating the rest as a retention-schedule/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA public-records determination."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"high","position_summary":"A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were \"public records\" under San Jose's content-based test (the subject matter — franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing, discussed by a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers — is core public business regardless of platform or device), and would very likely also find the clerk's city-systems-only search inadequate given a request that expressly demanded records \"on any platform or account.\" I moderate slightly from my Round 1 score of 80 to 78 because reconsideration of the panel's Theme G (a plausible, non-frivolous defense that the clerk lacked actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group and so the initial search was \"reasonable at the time\") and the genuine doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 (whether request language alone triggers a duty to inquire, versus knowledge being required) persuade me the second prong carries slightly more real litigation risk than I initially weighted — though I still think that defense is more likely to lose than win against the \"any platform or account\" language.","rationales":["San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 ties 'public record' status to content ('relates to the conduct of the public's business') rather than to the account, device, or platform where the writing sits, and the stipulated subject matter here — franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' RFP timing — is a paradigm case rather than a marginal one, especially with a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers as direct participants (Theme A/H, 7/7 and 3/7 panelists respectively).","The CPRA request's explicit 'on any platform or account' language, combined with the duty-to-assist/reasonable-diligence standard (Gov. Code former § 6253.1 / § 7922.600; see also ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 on 'reasonably calculated to locate responsive documents'), plausibly obligated the clerk to inquire of the named councilmembers about personal-account communications independent of whether the clerk had specific prior knowledge of the Signal group — a request-language-triggers-duty theory that 5/7 panelists endorsed (Theme C) and that I still find more persuasive than the competing knowledge-required theory.","The ~40 surviving screenshots are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed at the time of the request, making 'no responsive records' factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the destroyed messages — an essentially undisputed point across the panel (Theme D).","On reconsideration, Theme G's countervailing point (3/7 panelists: a court could find the initial search reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the private, consultant-run, ephemeral Signal group) and the unresolved doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 over what triggers the duty to inquire are legitimate sources of litigation risk I underweighted in Round 1; this is why I moved from 80 to 78 rather than holding at 80, even though I believe this defense is more likely to fail than succeed given the request's explicit multi-platform language.","The proposition is conjunctive (public-record status AND a violation from the no-inquiry response), and while I now peg each prong individually higher than my blended estimate might suggest (roughly ~90% for the public-records prong, ~85% for the search-violation prong), positive correlation between the two findings (a court persuaded of strong public-business content nexus is also more likely to be unsympathetic to a shortcut search) keeps the combined estimate in the high-70s rather than materially lower."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of 'public records')","Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — verify current recodified section number","Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction)","ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search-adequacy / 'reasonably calculated' standard) — verify continued applicability post-recodification","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision) — verify whether it establishes a custodian-inquiry/certification methodology applicable here","AB 700 personal-account search provisions — section number disputed among panelists (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5, Stats. 2021, ch. 132 or ch. 456); verify correct citation before relying on it"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat the request's 'any platform or account' language as itself sufficient to trigger a duty to inquire of individual councilmembers (making the city-systems-only search a violation as a matter of law), or will instead require actual or constructive notice of the specific Signal group before faulting the initial search — this doctrinal trigger question is genuinely unsettled post-San Jose and directly determines whether the second prong of the conjunctive proposition holds.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"I moved from 80 to 78 based on Theme G (clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense, raised by 3/7 panelists) together with Disagreement Point 1 (the unresolved doctrinal split over whether request-language alone or actual/constructive knowledge triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians). On reflection I think these are legitimate, non-frivolous considerations I underweighted in Round 1 — they represent a real, if likely losing, defense for the city on the second (search-violation) prong. I did not move further down because I remain persuaded the 'any platform or account' language argument (Theme C, 5/7 panelists) is doctrinally stronger than the knowledge-required counter-theory, and because Theme I (deleted-messages scope problem) was already built into my Round 1 reasoning as my own stated biggest uncertainty, so it didn't independently move me further. I did not defer to the panel median (78) merely for consensus's sake; I re-derived 78 independently by separately estimating the two conjunctive prongs (~90% and ~85%) and combining them with positive correlation."}

data.position_summary: A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test (the subject matter — franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing, discussed by a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers — is core public business regardless of platform or device), and would very likely also find the clerk's city-systems-only search inadequate given a request that expressly demanded records "on any platform or account." I moderate slightly from my Round 1 score of 80 to 78 because reconsideration of the panel's Theme G (a plausible, non-frivolous defense that the clerk lacked actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group and so the initial search was "reasonable at the time") and the genuine doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 (whether request language alone triggers a duty to inquire, versus knowledge being required) persuade me the second prong carries slightly more real litigation risk than I initially weighted — though I still think that defense is more likely to lose than win against the "any platform or account" language.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 ties 'public record' status to content ('relates to the conduct of the public's business') rather than to the account, device, or platform where the writing sits, and the stipulated subject matter here — franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' RFP timing — is a paradigm case rather than a marginal one, especially with a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers as direct participants (Theme A/H, 7/7 and 3/7 panelists respectively).

The CPRA request's explicit 'on any platform or account' language, combined with the duty-to-assist/reasonable-diligence standard (Gov. Code former § 6253.1 / § 7922.600; see also ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 on 'reasonably calculated to locate responsive documents'), plausibly obligated the clerk to inquire of the named councilmembers about personal-account communications independent of whether the clerk had specific prior knowledge of the Signal group — a request-language-triggers-duty theory that 5/7 panelists endorsed (Theme C) and that I still find more persuasive than the competing knowledge-required theory.

The ~40 surviving screenshots are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed at the time of the request, making 'no responsive records' factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the destroyed messages — an essentially undisputed point across the panel (Theme D).

On reconsideration, Theme G's countervailing point (3/7 panelists: a court could find the initial search reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the private, consultant-run, ephemeral Signal group) and the unresolved doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 over what triggers the duty to inquire are legitimate sources of litigation risk I underweighted in Round 1; this is why I moved from 80 to 78 rather than holding at 80, even though I believe this defense is more likely to fail than succeed given the request's explicit multi-platform language.

The proposition is conjunctive (public-record status AND a violation from the no-inquiry response), and while I now peg each prong individually higher than my blended estimate might suggest (roughly ~90% for the public-records prong, ~85% for the search-violation prong), positive correlation between the two findings (a court persuaded of strong public-business content nexus is also more likely to be unsympathetic to a shortcut search) keeps the combined estimate in the high-70s rather than materially lower.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — verify current recodified section number
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search-adequacy / 'reasonably calculated' standard) — verify continued applicability post-recodification
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision) — verify whether it establishes a custodian-inquiry/certification methodology applicable here

AB 700 personal-account search provisions — section number disputed among panelists (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5, Stats. 2021, ch. 132 or ch. 456); verify correct citation before relying on it
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the request's 'any platform or account' language as itself sufficient to trigger a duty to inquire of individual councilmembers (making the city-systems-only search a violation as a matter of law), or will instead require actual or constructive notice of the specific Signal group before faulting the initial search — this doctrinal trigger question is genuinely unsettled post-San Jose and directly determines whether the second prong of the conjunctive proposition holds.
data.response_to_panel: I moved from 80 to 78 based on Theme G (clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense, raised by 3/7 panelists) together with Disagreement Point 1 (the unresolved doctrinal split over whether request-language alone or actual/constructive knowledge triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians). On reflection I think these are legitimate, non-frivolous considerations I underweighted in Round 1 — they represent a real, if likely losing, defense for the city on the second (search-violation) prong. I did not move further down because I remain persuaded the 'any platform or account' language argument (Theme C, 5/7 panelists) is doctrinally stronger than the knowledge-required counter-theory, and because Theme I (deleted-messages scope problem) was already built into my Round 1 reasoning as my own stated biggest uncertainty, so it didn't independently move me further. I did not defer to the panel median (78) merely for consensus's sake; I re-derived 78 independently by separately estimating the two conjunctive prongs (~90% and ~85%) and combining them with positive correlation.
